questions.text: Receptor sites for neurotransmitters are present on the:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Presynaptic membrane has synaptic vesicles which contain neurotransmitter. When nerve impulse reaches the vesicles, neurotransmitter is released into the synaptic cleft.
Tips of axons have synaptic vesicles, which contain neurotransmitter.

A neurotransmitter is a chemical substance responsible for the transmission of nerve impulses across a synapse. It is released by the synaptic vesicle into the synaptic cleft. The neurotransmitter binds with protein receptor molecule present on the postsynaptic membrane causing its depolarization and generation of an action potential.
Synaptic vesicles contain neurotransmitter for transmission of impulse.

questions.explanation: A neurotransmitter is a chemical substance responsible for the transmission of nerve impulses across a synapse. It is released by the synaptic vesicle into the synaptic cleft. The neurotransmitter binds with protein receptor molecule present on the postsynaptic membrane causing its depolarization and generation of an action potential.

questions.text: Which of the following regions of the brain is incorrectly paired with its function?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Corpus callosum - communication between the left and right cerebral cortices
options.explanation: The corpus callosum facilitates communication between the left and right hemispheres of the brain, allowing for coordinated functions.
The cerebrum is responsible for higher cognitive functions, including calculation, contemplation, and other complex thought processes.
The medulla oblongata plays a crucial role in maintaining homeostasis by regulating vital functions such as heart rate and breathing.
The cerebellum is primarily involved in motor control and coordination, not language comprehension, which is a function of the cerebrum. This makes it the incorrect pairing.

questions.explanation: The cerebellum coordinates movement and balance. Language comprehension is a function of the cerebrum.

questions.text: Injury localized to the hypothalamus would most likely disrupt:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Short-term memory is mainly associated with the hippocampus in the temporal lobe, not the hypothalamus.

Coordination during locomotion is controlled mainly by the cerebellum, not the hypothalamus. The cerebellum integrates sensory input with motor commands to maintain balance, posture, and smooth, coordinated movements while walking or running.

options.text: Executive functions; such as decision making
options.explanation: Executive functions such as decision-making, planning, and judgment are primarily controlled by the frontal lobe of the cerebrum, especially the prefrontal cortex. The hypothalamus, on the other hand, mainly regulates autonomic functions like hunger, thirst, temperature, and hormone release, not higher executive tasks.

The hypothalamus lies at the base of the thalamus. It provides an anatomical connection between the nervous and endocrine systems through its relationship to the pituitary gland. The hypothalamus is a thermoregulatory centre. Hence, it is called the “thermostat” of the body. It keeps body temperature at roughly 37°C by means of a complex thermostat system. Any localised injury to the hypothalamus will, hence, disrupt the regulation of body temperature.

questions.explanation: The hypothalamus lies at the base of the thalamus. It provides an anatomical connection between the nervous and endocrine systems through its relationship to the pituitary gland. The hypothalamus is a thermoregulatory centre. Hence, it is called the “thermostat” of the body. It keeps body temperature at roughly 37°C by means of a complex thermostat system. Any localised injury to the hypothalamus will, hence, disrupt the regulation of body temperature.

questions.text: The figure shows an axon terminal and synapse. Select the option giving correct identification of labels A to ­D.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A is action potential but C is receptor. An action potential is a rapid electrical change in a neuron’s membrane that transmits a nerve impulse. A receptor is a structure that detects a stimulus and generates a nerve impulse.

B is neurotransmitter but D is synaptic vesicle. A neurotransmitter is a chemical messenger that transmits signals between neurons across a synapse. A synaptic vesicle is a tiny sac that stores and releases neurotransmitters at the synapse.

This is the correct option as C shows the receptor which receives the nerve impulse and D shows the synaptic vesicles which release neurotransmitter, helping to propagate the impulse aling the synapse.

Incorrect because A is action potential and B is neurotransmitter. Axon terminal is the forms the presynaptic membrane and brings the impulse towards the synapse. Serotonin complex is a type of neurotransmitter.

questions.explanation: A is action potential, B is neurotransmitter, C is receptor and D is synaptic vesicles. An action potential is a rapid electrical change in a neuron’s membrane that transmits a nerve impulse. A neurotransmitter is a chemical messenger that transmits signals between neurons across a synapse. A receptor is a structure that detects a stimulus and generates a nerve impulse. A synaptic vesicle is a tiny sac that stores and releases neurotransmitters at the synapse.

questions.text: When a neuron is in the resting state i.e. not conducting any impulse then, the axonal membrane is _.
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Comparatively more permeable to Na+ ions and nearly impermeable to K+ ions
options.explanation: This is incorrect because during the resting state, the axonal membrane is actually more permeable to K+ ions, not Na+ ions.

options.text: Equally permeable to both Na+ and K+ ions
options.explanation: This option is incorrect as only K+ channels are open during the resting state; Na+ channels are closed.

options.text: Impermeable to both Na+ and K+ ions
options.explanation: This is not true because the axonal membrane is permeable to K+ ions due to open leak channels.

options.text: Comparatively more permeable to K+ ions and nearly impermeable to Na+ ions
options.explanation: This is the correct answer as during the resting state, the axonal membrane allows more movement of K+ ions while restricting Na+ ions due to the presence of K+ leak channels.

questions.explanation: At rest, K+ leak channels remain open----> membrane is more permeable to K+, while Na+ channels are closed----> inside of axon is negative.

questions.text: Which one of the following does not act as a neurotransmitter?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cortisone is a pregnene steroid hormone. It is a naturally-occurring corticosteroid metabolite that is also used as a pharmaceutical prodrug.

Acetylcholine is an organic compound that functions in the brain and body of many types of animals as a neurotransmitter. Its name is derived from its chemical structure: it is an ester of acetic acid and choline. Parts in the body that use or are affected by acetylcholine are referred to as cholinergic.

Adrenaline, also known as epinephrine, is a hormone and medication which is involved in regulating visceral functions. It appears as a white microcrystalline granule. Adrenaline is normally produced by the adrenal glands and by a small number of neurons in the medulla oblongata.
Norepinephrine, also called noradrenaline or noradrenalin, is an organic chemical in the catecholamine family that functions in the brain and body as both a hormone and neurotransmitter.

questions.explanation: Neurotransmitters are chemicals that are used to relay, amplify, and modulate electrical signals between a neuron and another cell. Substances that act as neurotransmitters can be categorized into three major groups: (1) amino acids (primarily glutamic acid, GABA, aspartic acid, and glycine), (2) peptides (vasopressin, somatostatin, neurotensin, etc.), and (3) monoamines (norepinephrine, dopamine, and serotonin) plus acetylcholine. Cortisone is a glucocorticoid steroid hormone produced by the adrenal glands and has anti-inflammatory and immunosuppressant properties.

questions.text: Which of the following statements is correct for the the node of Ranvier of the nerve?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Neurilemma is the membrane of the nerve and provides a base for myelin sheaths and nodes of ranvier. However neurilemma is not continuous.

At the level of the node of Ranvier, the myelin sheath is discontinuous but not the neurilemma lining. Actually, the myelin sheath is an integral part of the Schwann cell – which forms a continuous neurilemmal covering. Each Schwann cell wraps around the neurite to form concentric layers of the plasma membrane. But at the level of the junction between two Schwann cells, myelin cannot be formed and thus a gap appears.

options.text: Both neurilemma and myelin sheath are discontinuous.
options.explanation: Incorrect because neurilemma is not discontinuous. It is the covering of the nerve membrane. But myelin sheath is discontinuous as nerve impulse needs to be propagated through nodes of ranvier.

options.text: It is covered by the myelin sheath
options.explanation: It is not totally covered by myelin sheath, instead is discontinuous and contains nodes of ranvier through which nerve impulses travel.

questions.explanation: At the level of the node of Ranvier, the myelin sheath is discontinuous but not the neurilemma lining. Actually, the myelin sheath is an integral part of the Schwann cell – which forms a continuous neurilemmal covering. Each Schwann cell wraps around the neurite to form concentric layers of the plasma membrane. But at the level of the junction between two Schwann cells, myelin cannot be formed and thus a gap appears.

questions.text: The sympathetic nervous system induces:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Secretion of digestive juices is induced by parasympathetic nervous systems in relaxed state unlike sympathetic nervous system.

Most ganglion fibres of the sympathetic system arise from the middle portion of the spinal cord and almost terminate in ganglia that lie near the cord. This system is important during emergency situations and is associated with “fight or flight.” This system accelerates the heart beat, dilates the pupil and inhibits the digestion of food etc.
The sympathetic nervous system causes the secretion of small amounts of thick saliva, while the parasympathetic nervous system produces large amounts of watery saliva. So this is partially incorrect.
This is incorrect as increased heart rate is a response to fight or flight which is induced by the sympathetic nervous system.

questions.explanation: The sympathetic nervous system is a component of the autonomic nervous system consisting of a pair of sympathetic trunks, preganglionic sympathetic fibres, postganglionic sympathetic fibres and collateral ganglia. It quickens the rate and force of heartbeat while it inhibits the secretion of saliva and gastric juice. It prepares the body for fight or flight.

questions.text: The junction between the axon of one neuron and the dendrite of the next is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A constant bridge is not a recognized term in neuroscience. It suggests a stable connection, which does not apply to the dynamic interaction between neurons.
While this term indicates a meeting place, it lacks the specificity needed in neuroanatomy. The proper term for this connection is different and more widely accepted.
A joint typically refers to a connection between bones in the skeletal system. It is not applicable when discussing the interaction between neurons.
A synapse is the correct term that describes the junction where the axon terminal of one neuron meets the dendrite of another, allowing for signal transmission through neurotransmitter release.

questions.explanation: A synapse is the functional junction between two neurons separated by a tiny synaptic cleft (-200 A). Neurotransmitters released from the presynaptic membrane cross this gap to transmit the nerve impulse to the postsynaptic neuron.

questions.text: Norepinephrine (i)is released by sympathetic fibers (ii)is released by parasympathetic fibers (iii)increases the heart rate (iv)decreases blood pressureWhich of the above statements are correct?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Sympathetic fibers are also called small-fiber nerves. It is developed from the collections of nerve clusters called ganglia. It is available on the outside of the spinal cord. It will increase the rate of heartbeat, muscle contraction, and rate of conduction. So, i is correct. And iii is also correct.

The parasympathetic nervous system consists of cranial nerves, the vagus nerve, and the lumbar spinal nerves. During this nerve stimulation, it increases digestive secretions and also reduces heart rate. The norepinephrine hormone will increase the heartbeat. So, ii is not correct and iii is correct.
Sympathetic fibers release the norepinephrine. Blood pressure is increased during the large amount of norepinephrine hormone excretion. So, i is correct but iv is not the correct answer.
Parasympathetic fibers do not produce the norepinephrine hormone. Decreasing blood pressure is not possible during the excretion of hormones. So, both ii and iv are not correct.

questions.explanation: Norepinephrine is secreted by some neurons of the sympathetic nervous system and also by the adrenal medulla. It accelerates the heart rate. Norepinephrine is a hormone and neurotransmitter that primarily functions to mediate the body's "fight-or-flight" response to stress by increasing heart rate, blood pressure, and blood flow to muscles and the brain. It also plays a crucial role in the brain by regulating attention, focus, alertness, and the sleep-wake cycle. Additionally, it affects mood and memory and is involved in energy regulation through the breakdown of stored fats and glycogen for glucose.

questions.text: Low Ca2+ in the body fluid may be the cause of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Low levels of blood Ca++ ions stimulate the parathyroid directly to increase parathormone production, whereas high levels of Ca++ ions suppress its release. Underactivity causes a drop in blood Ca++ ions, which in turn leads to muscular tetany.

Anemia is a condition in which the number of red blood cells (RBCs) or the amount of hemoglobin in them is lower than normal, reducing the blood’s capacity to carry oxygen. This results in symptoms such as fatigue, weakness, pale skin, dizziness, and shortness of breath.
Angina pectoris—or simply angina—is chest pain or discomfort that keeps coming back.

Gout is a common and complex form of arthritis that can affect anyone. It's characterized by sudden, severe attacks of pain, swelling, redness, and tenderness in one or more joints, most often in the big toe.

questions.explanation: Tetany is a spasm and twitching of the muscles, particularly those of the face, hands, and feet. Tetany is usually caused by a reduction in the blood calcium level, which may be due to underactive parathyroid glands, rickets, or alkalosis.

questions.text: A motor nerve carries impulses from:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Motor neurons are part of the spinal nerves. Spinal nerves carry the motor neurons to the effectors. This option is very vague, hence incorrect.
Effectors do not carry messages to the CNS. Rather, it is the other way around as the CNS needs to give command to the effectors(i.e. muscle or gland). Cranial nerves are part of the CNS.
Effectors do not carry messages to the CNS. Rather, it is the other way around from CNS to effectors as it gives commands to the effector to start working.

There are three types of neurons:Motor neurons carry the impulse from CNS to the Effectors.Intermediate neurons connect the Sensory and motor neuron present in CNSSensory neurons carry the impulse from receptors to the CNS.Effectors are the organs that receive command from CNS to take action in response to a stimulus. Receptors are either special cells or Dendrites of Sensory neurons that detect the stimulus.

questions.explanation: Motor neurons are meant to conduct messages from CNS to the effectors as they are emerging from CNS like glands or muscles and stimulates them to work (secrete hormones or contract). That is why they are known as motor neurons.Motor neurons carry electrical impulses from the Central Nervous System (CNS) to effectors (muscles or glands) by transmitting signals through their cell body, a long axon, and terminal branches to the effector's muscle fibers or gland cells. The signal travels as an electrical impulse along the axon, which may be insulated by a myelin sheath to speed up transmission. At the axon terminal, neurotransmitters are released, crossing the synapse to trigger a response in the effector, such as muscle contraction or hormone release. Hence option D is correct.

questions.text: Pressure is felt by your body immediately after it is appIied. This is achieved with the help of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: These are the receptors present in the skin dermis for the detection of Pressure and touch. They have free nerve endings.
These are present in the skin dermis for detection of change in temperature. They include ruffini ending for heat and krause end bulbs for cold.
These are the receptors in the skin that detect the pain. These are located in the epidermis of the skin. They are present throughout the body except in brain.

These are present in the taste buds. The tongue is covered with small bumps called papillae. Each papillae has many taste buds. The taste buds contain 50 to 100 Chemoreceptors that detect the chemical in the liquid we drink. The Chemoreceptors are covered with receptors proteins that detect these different chemicals.

questions.explanation: Mechanoreceptors detect stimuli of touch, pressure, hearing and equilibrium.They have free nerve endings.

questions.text: Which of the following produce response?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Effectors are the organs, such as muscles or glands, that respond to signals from the central nervous system (CNS) to carry out actions in reaction to stimuli.
Nerves are the structures that transmit signals within the nervous system, but they do not produce a response themselves; rather, they relay information to effectors.
Stimulators refer to devices or methods used to provoke a response in the nervous system but do not themselves produce a response. They assist in the functioning of effectors.
The brain is responsible for processing information and sending commands to effectors, but it does not directly produce a response; it coordinates the response through effectors.

questions.explanation: Effectors are organs like muscles or glands that produce a response to a stimulus by contracting or secreting substances when signaled by the nervous or endocrine system.

questions.text: Myelinated, single and long fiber that takes message towards the cell body is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A dendron is a long, myelinated protoplasmic extension of a neuron that conducts electrochemical signals towards the cell body. It is distinguished from dendrites, which are shorter and branch off from the dendron.
An axon is a long, myelinated nerve fiber that transmits impulses away from the cell body to other neurons or effectors. Therefore, it does not carry messages towards the cell body.
Dendrites are short, branched extensions of a neuron that receive signals from other neurons and carry them towards the cell body. However, they are not typically classified as a single long fiber.
The soma, or cell body, is part of the neuron that contains the nucleus and organelles. It does not function as a fiber that transmits messages.

questions.explanation: Dendron is a single, long protoplasmic extension of a neuron that carries impulses toward the cell body. Smaller branches from dendron are called dendrites.

questions.text: Which of the following is not correct about myelin sheath?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The myelin sheath is indeed composed of specialized lipids, primarily sphingomyelin, that form a protective and insulating layer around the axon. This structure is crucial for its function.
The myelin sheath facilitates saltatory conduction, allowing nerve impulses to jump between nodes of Ranvier, thereby increasing the speed of impulse transmission significantly.
This statement is incorrect because the myelin sheath itself does not conduct impulses. Instead, it insulates the axon, which is where the actual conduction of impulses occurs.
This statement is true; the myelin sheath is absent at the nodes of Ranvier. These nodes are essential for the generation of new action potentials as ions flow across the membrane.

questions.explanation: The myelin sheath does not conduct impulses. It insulates the axon, speeding conduction through saltatory conduction. at nodes of Ranvier, but impulses themselves travel along the axon.

questions.text: Which of the following is most abundant type of neurons in CNS?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Sensory neurons are specialized for transmitting information from sensory organs to the CNS. They are actually the least abundant type of neuron in the CNS, accounting for approximately 10% of the total neuronal population.

Also known as interneurons, associative neurons are the most abundant neurons in the CNS. They facilitate communication between sensory and motor neurons and are integral to complex functions such as learning, memory, and decision-making.
Motor neurons transmit signals from the CNS to muscles, enabling movement. They are significantly less abundant than interneurons, constituting about 1% of the total neuron population in the CNS.

Neuroglial cells are supportive cells in the CNS, not neurons. While they outnumber neurons by about 10 to 1, they do not directly participate in neuronal signaling, so they cannot be classified as neurons.

questions.explanation: Interneurons (associative neurons) are the most abundant. neurons in the CNS. They process and relay information between sensory and motor neurons and are crucial for higher-order functions like learning, memory, and decision-making.

questions.text: Which of the following is not a part of neuron?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The axon is a long, thin fiber that carries electrical impulses away from the neuron's cell body, playing a crucial role in transmitting signals. It is definitely a part of a neuron.
Dendrites are short, branching extensions of a neuron that receive incoming signals from other neurons. They are essential components of neurons.
Nissl granules are clusters of ribosomes found within the cell body of a neuron, involved in the synthesis of proteins. They are part of the neuron structure.
The synapse is the junction where two neurons communicate, involving the transmission of signals. However, it is not a structural component of a single neuron, making it the correct answer here.

questions.explanation: A synapse is the junction between two neurons for signal transmission and is not part of the neuron itself. Axons. dendrites, and Nissl granules are structural components of a neuron.

questions.text: Which one of the following is common to all neurons?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: A cell body which contains a nucleus
options.explanation: The cell body, or soma, is crucial for all neurons as it contains the nucleus, which holds the genetic material and regulates cellular functions. This structure is key for neuronal maintenance and growth, making it present in every neuron.

Nodes of Ranvier are gaps in the myelin sheath that are found in myelinated neurons, allowing for faster signal transmission. However, not all neurons are myelinated, and thus, some do not have these nodes, making this option incorrect.

Myelin sheaths insulate axons and enhance the speed of impulse conduction. However, the thickness of the myelin sheath varies among neurons, and some are unmyelinated. Therefore, this characteristic is not universal among all neurons.

Schwann cells are responsible for the formation of the myelin sheath around peripheral nerves. While they play a critical role in myelination, not all neurons are associated with Schwann cells, particularly those in the central nervous system, making this option incorrect.

questions.explanation: The cell body (soma) is the main nutritional and metabolic center of all neurons, containing the nucleus and responsible for biosynthesis of proteins and energy for neuronal function.

questions.text: _ is considered as chief structural and functional unit of nervous system.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The term 'cell' is too general. While it encompasses various types of cells, it does not specify the specialized cell type crucial for the nervous system's function.
Neurons are indeed the chief structural and functional units of the nervous system, responsible for transmitting signals and processing information.
Nephrons are the functional units of the kidney, not the nervous system, and therefore do not relate to this question.
While the brain is a critical component of the nervous system, it is not the only structure involved. Neurons, which are found throughout the nervous system, are the fundamental units.

questions.explanation: The neuron is the chief structural and functional unit of the nervous system. It transmits information through electrical and chemical signals and is responsible for sensations, thoughts, and movements.

questions.text: Which of the following is not a component of reflex arc?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The afferent nerve, also known as a sensory neuron, carries signals from sensory receptors to the spinal cord. This component is crucial for initiating the reflex response.
The brain processes information but is not part of the reflex arc itself. Reflex arcs operate primarily at the spinal cord level for quick responses, bypassing the brain in immediate reflex actions.
Efferent nerves, or motor neurons, transmit impulses from the spinal cord to effectors, facilitating the body's response to stimuli. This component is essential for the completion of the reflex arc.
Effectors are the muscles or glands that act in response to the impulses received from motor neurons. They are a critical part of the reflex arc, executing the response to the stimulus.

questions.explanation: A reflex arc consists of receptors, sensory neurons. intermeurons. motor neurons, and effectors. The brain is not involved in the simplest reflex arc.

questions.text: Which of the following describes the comprehensive pathway of reflex arc?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Prick on finger → Sensory nerve → Medulla → Motor nerve → Arm Muscle
options.explanation: The simplest arrangement of a reflex consists of the receptors, an interneuron, and an effector; together these units form a functional reflex group. The brain is, however, excluded in the reflex arc. The medulla, Cerebrum, and Cerebellum are parts of the brain.

options.text: Prick on finger → Sensory nerve → Spinal cord → Motor nerve → Finger muscles
options.explanation: This simple reflex circuit includes each of the four elements of a neural pathway. (1) The sensoryneuron has pain-sensitive endings in the skin and a long fiber leading to the spinal cord. Thatneuron stimulates (2) an association neuron in the spinal cord, which in turn stimulates (3) a motorneuron, also in the cord. The axon of the motor neuron carries action potentials to (4) muscles,causing them to contract and withdraw the body part from the damaging stimulus. The sensoryneuron also makes a synapse on association neurons not involved in the reflex that carry signals tothe brain, informing it of the danger.

options.text: Prick on finger → Sensory nerve → Cerebrum → Motor nerve → Finger muscles
options.explanation: The simplest arrangement of a reflex are consists of the receptors, an interneuron, and an effector; together these units from a finctional reflex group. Brain is, however, excluded in the reflex arc. Medulla, Cerebrum and Cerebellum are parts of the brain.

options.text: Prick on finger → Sensory nerve → Cerebellum → Motor nerve → Finger muscles
options.explanation: The simplest arrangement of a reflex are consists of the receptors, an interneuron, and an effector; together these units from a finctional reflex group. Brain is, however, excluded in the reflex arc. Medulla, Cerebrum and Cerebellum are parts of the brain.

questions.explanation: The simplest arrangement of a reflex are consists of the receptors, an interneuron, and an effector; together these units from a finctional reflex group.A reflex arc is the neural pathway followed by nerve impulses during a reflex action, providing a quick, involuntary, and automatic response to a stimulus without conscious thought. It typically involves a receptor to detect the stimulus, a sensory neuron to carry the signal to the spinal cord, an interneuron (in most cases) in the spinal cord, a motor neuron to transmit the signal to an effector, and an effector (like a muscle) that produces the response. Brain is, however, excluded in the reflex arc. Medulla, Cerebrum and Cerebellum are parts of the brain.

questions.text: Depolarization is the result of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The influx of potassium ions (K+) into a cell does not lead to depolarization. It can have the opposite effect, contributing to repolarization or hyperpolarization.

Depolarization is primarily the result of an influx of sodium ions (Na+). This influx of positively charged sodium ions into a cell, particularly a neuron, reduces the cell's membrane potential, making it less negative. This change in membrane potential is a key step in the initiation of an action potential.
Efflux of potassium ions (K+) can contribute to repolarization, as potassium ions leaving the cell make the membrane potential more negative, bringing it closer to its resting state.
Efflux of sodium ions (Na+) would not contribute to depolarization. Sodium ions leaving the cell would tend to restore the resting membrane potential.

questions.explanation: During depolarization, the neuron’s voltage-gated sodium (Na⁺) channels open in response to a stimulus.Na⁺ ions then flow into the cell because their concentration is higher outside and the inside is negatively charged.This influx of positive ions causes the membrane potential to rise from about –70 mV (resting potential) toward +30 mV, making the inside less negative and more positive. This is the depolarization phase of the action potential.

questions.text: Which of the following will restore original ion gradients and resting membrane potential (RMP) after hyperpolarization in neurons?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Potassium channels are primarily responsible for repolarizing the neuron during an action potential. However, they do not restore the original ion gradients or resting membrane potential after hyperpolarization, as their primary role is to facilitate the outflow of K+ ions.

The sodium-potassium pump is crucial for restoring the original ionic gradients after hyperpolarization by actively transporting Na+ ions out of the cell and K+ ions back in, thus re-establishing the resting membrane potential.

Sodium channels facilitate depolarization during action potentials by allowing Na+ ions to enter the neuron. They do not contribute to restoring the resting membrane potential after hyperpolarization.
Calcium channels play roles in various cellular processes, including neurotransmitter release, but they are not involved in restoring the resting membrane potential after hyperpolarization.

questions.explanation: In myelinated neurons, action potentials jump from one node of Ranvier to the next. Myelin acts as insulation, speeding up conduction by preventing continuous depolarization along the axon.

questions.text: Conduction of action potentials from one node of Ranvier to another in myelinated neurons is through:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Hyperpolarization describes a state where the membrane potential becomes more negative than the resting potential, reducing neuronal excitability. This process is not involved in the conduction of action potentials in myelinated neurons.

Depolarization is an essential phase of action potential generation, where the membrane potential becomes less negative. However, it does not describe the method of action potential propagation between nodes in myelinated neurons.

The resting membrane potential is the stable, baseline voltage across a neuron's membrane. While crucial for neuronal function, it does not explain the mechanism of action potential transmission in myelinated neurons.

Saltatory conduction is the process by which action potentials jump from one node of Ranvier to another in myelinated neurons. This method of conduction increases the speed and efficiency of nerve impulse transmission, making it the correct answer.

questions.explanation: In myelinated neurons, action potentials jump from one node of Ranvier to the next. Myelin acts as insulation, speeding up conduction by preventing continuous depolarization along the axon.

questions.text: How many sodium ions are pumped out in response to two potassium ions transported into the membrane?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because the sodium-potassium pump transports three sodium ions out of the cell for every two potassium ions it brings in. Therefore, four sodium ions do not correspond to the correct ratio of ion transport.

This option is incorrect as it misrepresents the function of the sodium-potassium pump. The pump actually moves three sodium ions out of the cell in exchange for two potassium ions coming in, not just one sodium ion.

This choice is inaccurate because the sodium-potassium pump actively transports three sodium ions out while taking in two potassium ions, not two sodium ions. Thus, the number provided does not reflect the correct ionic exchange ratio.

This option is correct because the sodium-potassium pump actively expels three sodium ions out of the cell while simultaneously transporting two potassium ions into the cell. This process is essential for maintaining the electrochemical gradient necessary for cellular functions.

questions.explanation: The sodium-potassium pump moves 3 Na+ out and 2 K+ in per cycle, maintaining the resting membrane potential (-70 mV) with more positive charge outside than inside.

questions.text: In an action potential, the permeability of sodium ions in the neurons increases due to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Option A refers to repolarization, which is the phase where potassium ions exit the neuron, leading to a decrease in membrane potential. During this phase, the permeability of sodium ions does not increase, making this option incorrect.
Option B discusses ionic bonding, which is not relevant to the action potential process. The increase in sodium permeability is due to channel opening, not bonding.

Option C is correct. During an action potential, specifically during depolarization, the opening of voltage-gated sodium channels allows sodium ions to flow into the neuron, significantly increasing the permeability to sodium.

options.text: The action of the acetylcholinesterase enzyme
options.explanation: Option D refers to acetylcholinesterase, which breaks down acetylcholine in the synaptic cleft, but does not impact sodium permeability in neurons during an action potential.

questions.explanation: Voltage-gated Na channels open during depolarization. allowing sodium ions to flow into the neuron. This influx of positive ions makes the inside of the cell less negative and triggers the action potential.

questions.text: If stimulation is above _, impulses travel to the brain along the sensory neuron.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because an action potential is a rapid change in the membrane potential of a neuron that occurs once the threshold is reached, allowing the neuron to transmit signals, but it doesn't indicate the level of stimulation required for impulses to travel to the brain along a sensory neuron.

This option is incorrect as resting potential refers to the stable voltage across a neuron's membrane when it's not transmitting signals, and it doesn't specify the threshold needed for impulses to travel to the brain via sensory neurons.

This option is correct because the threshold is the minimum level of stimulation required for a neuron to generate an action potential. If the stimulation is above the threshold, impulses can travel to the brain along sensory neurons, initiating the transmission of sensory information.

This option is incorrect because the recovery period is a phase after an action potential when a neuron is temporarily less responsive to new stimuli, but it doesn't determine the threshold for impulses to travel to the brain through sensory neurons.

questions.explanation: The threshold is the minimum level of stimulation required for a neuron to generate an action potential. If the stimulation is above the threshold, impulses can travel to the brain along sensory neurons, initiating the transmission of sensory information.

questions.text: Which of the following diagram best shows the resting membrane potential?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Not correct because during resting membrane potential, membrane will be highly positive from outside and highly negative from inside.
Correct because during resting membrane potential, membrane will be highly positive from outside and highly negative from inside.
Not correct because during resting membrane potential, membrane will be highly positive from outside and highly negative from inside, not half positive or half negative.
Not correct because during resting membrane potential, membrane will be highly positive from outside and highly negative from inside, not half positive or half negative.

questions.explanation: During resting membrane potential, membrane will be highly positive from outside and highly negative from inside due to the concentration of K+ and Na+ ions. The resting membrane potential (RMP) is the electrical potential difference across a cell's plasma membrane when the cell is in a non-excited state. This negative charge inside the cell, typically ranging from −70 mV to −90 mV for neurons, is maintained by the sodium-potassium pump, leakage of potassium ions out of the cell, and the presence of large negative anions within the cell. The RMP allows cells, especially neurons, to be ready to transmit signals when stimulated.

questions.text: Microscopic gap between the neurons is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. A synapse encompasses the entire junction between two neurons, which includes the presynaptic terminal, the synaptic cleft, and the postsynaptic membrane. The question specifies a 'microscopic gap,' which is not the full definition of a synapse.

This option is correct. The synaptic cleft is the tiny gap between the presynaptic terminal of one neuron and the postsynaptic membrane of another neuron. It is crucial for neurotransmitter release and communication between neurons, making it the precise term for the 'microscopic gap' mentioned in the question.
This option is incorrect. 'Collapse' does not pertain to the anatomical structures of neurons and does not describe any part of neuronal communication, especially not the gap between neurons.
This option is incorrect. The term 'pre-synapse membrane' refers specifically to the membrane of the presynaptic neuron, which is where neurotransmitters are released, rather than the gap itself.

questions.explanation: The synaptic cleft is a tiny gap between the presynaptic terminal of one neuron and the postsynaptic membrane of another, where neurotransmitters are released to transmit the signal.

questions.text: Which ion is involved in the release of Acetylcholine from the pre-synaptic filter into extracellular fluid?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The release of acetylcholine from the pre-synaptic neuron into the extracellular fluid at the synapse is primarily triggered by the influx of calcium ions (Ca2+) into the pre-synaptic terminal. Calcium plays a crucial role in the fusion of synaptic vesicles containing acetylcholine with the cell membrane, allowing acetylcholine to be released into the synaptic cleft.

This option is incorrect. Potassium ions are not directly involved in the release of neurotransmitters. Instead, potassium ions are typically involved in the repolarization of the neuron's membrane after an action potential.
This option is incorrect. Sodium ions are not directly responsible for the release of neurotransmitters. Sodium ions are involved in depolarization and initiation of action potentials.

This option is incorrect. Magnesium ions are not involved in the release of neurotransmitters. They have a regulatory role in blocking calcium channels under certain conditions but are not the primary ions responsible for neurotransmitter release.

questions.explanation: Calcium ions (Ca2+) are involved in the release of neurotransmitters, including acetylcholine, from the presynaptic neuron into the synaptic cleft. When an action potential reaches the presynaptic terminal, it triggers the opening of voltage-gated calcium channels. This influx of calcium ions into the presynaptic terminal leads to the fusion of vesicles containing acetylcholine with the cell membrane and the subsequent release of acetylcholine into the extracellular fluid. The released acetylcholine molecules then bind to receptors on the postsynaptic membrane, initiating a response in the postsynaptic neuron.

questions.text: After the depolarization of postsynaptic neurolemma, neurotransmitters are mostly:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Schwann cells are primarily involved in the formation of myelin sheaths in the peripheral nervous system and supporting neurons. They are not typically responsible for the uptake of neurotransmitters in the synaptic cleft.

This option is incorrect. While some neurotransmitter molecules do remain in the synaptic cleft after release, this is not the primary fate of neurotransmitters following depolarization of the postsynaptic neurolemma.

This option is correct. Neurotransmitters that have been released into the synaptic cleft are often degraded by specific enzymes present in the cleft. Enzymes like acetylcholinesterase break down neurotransmitters like acetylcholine, terminating the signal transmission and allowing the synapse to reset for the next potential signal.

options.text: Taken up by post synaptic membrane
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. While there are mechanisms for neurotransmitter reuptake in some synapses, it is not the primary way neurotransmitters are cleared from the synaptic cleft after depolarization of the postsynaptic neurolemma.

questions.explanation: Neurotransmitters that have been released into the synaptic cleft are often degraded by specific enzymes present in the cleft. Enzymes like acetylcholinesterase break down neurotransmitters like acetylcholine, terminating the signal transmission and allowing the synapse to reset for the next potential signal. Neurotransmitters are degraded by specific enzymes, such as acetylcholinesterase and monoamine oxidase, which break them down into inactive components in the synaptic cleft. This enzymatic destruction changes the neurotransmitter's structure, preventing it from binding to receptors and signaling the next neuron. Once degraded, the resulting components can no longer stimulate the postsynaptic cell, ensuring the signal is terminated and preventing neuronal overstimulation.

questions.text: In nervous system, chemical messengers are called _.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Enzymes are proteins that catalyze chemical reactions in the body, but they are not the primary chemical messengers involved in transmitting signals in the nervous system.

This option is incorrect. Chemoreceptors are specialized sensory receptors that detect changes in chemical concentrations, such as those related to smell and taste. They are not the chemical messengers themselves.
This option is correct. In the nervous system, chemical messengers that transmit signals between neurons or from neurons to other cells, such as muscle cells, are called "neurotransmitters."

This option is incorrect. Hormones are chemical messengers produced by endocrine glands and released into the bloodstream to regulate various physiological processes in the body. While they play a role in communication and coordination, they are not specific to the nervous system and are primarily associated with the endocrine system.

questions.explanation: In the nervous system, chemical messengers that transmit signals between neurons or from neurons to other cells, such as muscle cells, are called "neurotransmitters." Neurotransmitters are released from the presynaptic neuron's terminal into the synaptic cleft, where they bind to receptors on the postsynaptic membrane and initiate a response in the receiving cell.

questions.text: Grey matter is composed of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: No, grey matter is not composed of Schwann cells. Schwann cells are neuroglial cells that are found in the peripheral nervous system (PNS). They are responsible for myelinating axons in the PNS. Myelin is a fatty sheath that surrounds axons and helps to increase the speed of nerve conduction.

No, grey matter is not composed of neuron fibers. Grey matter is composed of neuronal cell bodies, dendrites, and synapses. Neuron fibers, also known as axons, are long, thin extensions of neuronal cell bodies that carry signals to other neurons. Axons are not found in grey matter, but they are found in white matter.

Grey matter is composed of neuronal cell bodies, dendrites, and synapses. Neuronal cell bodies are the "brains" of neurons, where information is processed and stored. Dendrites are the "arms" of neurons, which receive signals from other neurons. Synapses are the junctions between neurons, where signals are passed from one neuron to another.

Nissl's granules are small, spherical bodies that are found in the cytoplasm of neurons. They are made up of rough endoplasmic reticulum (RER), which is a network of membranes that are involved in protein synthesis. Nissl's granules are therefore important for the production of proteins, which are essential for the function of neurons.

questions.explanation: Option C is correct.Grey matter is composed of neuronal cell bodies, dendrites, and synapses. Neuronal cell bodies are the "brains" of neurons, where information is processed and stored. Dendrites are the "arms" of neurons, which receive signals from other neurons. Synapses are the junctions between neurons, where signals are passed from one neuron to another. Grey matter is found in the cerebral cortex, the cerebellum, and the brainstem. The cerebral cortex is the outer layer of the brain and is responsible for higher-order functions, such as thinking, planning, and language. The cerebellum is responsible for coordination and balance. The brainstem is responsible for basic functions, such as breathing and heart rate.

questions.text: Preparing the body for the "fight-or-flight" response is the role of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The sympathetic nervous system is responsible for preparing the body for the "fight-or-flight" response. It activates physiological changes to help the body respond to stress or danger, such as increased heart rate, dilated pupils, and increased alertness.
The parasympathetic nervous system has the opposite role; it promotes the "rest and digest" response, helping the body to relax and conserve energy.

While both the sympathetic and parasympathetic nervous systems play important roles in regulating the body's autonomic functions, they have opposing effects. The sympathetic system prepares for action, while the parasympathetic system promotes relaxation and recovery.
This is not the correct answer, as the sympathetic nervous system is indeed responsible for the "fight-or-flight" response.

questions.explanation: The sympathetic nervous system is responsible for preparing the body for the "fight-or-flight" response. The sympathetic nervous system is one of the two branches of the autonomic nervous system, which is responsible for involuntary functions such as heart rate, breathing, and digestion.The sympathetic nervous system is activated in response to stress or danger. When the sympathetic nervous system is activated, it causes several changes in the body, including increased heart rate, widened pupils, sweating and muscle tension.

questions.text: Which part of a neuron may act as a receptor as well?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Dendrites are branch-like structures extending from the cell body of a neuron. They receive signals from other neurons and act as receptors for neurotransmitters, allowing them to receive incoming signals and pass them to the cell body.

The cell body contains the nucleus and other cellular organelles. While it plays a crucial role in integrating signals received from dendrites, it does not act as a receptor. Instead, it processes incoming signals and generates outgoing signals through the axon.

The axon is a long, slender projection that carries electrical impulses away from the cell body to other neurons, muscles, or glands. It does not act as a receptor but functions primarily to transmit signals.

A ganglion is a cluster of nerve cell bodies found outside the central nervous system (CNS). While ganglia can be involved in receiving and processing signals, they are not part of an individual neuron, and their primary function is not acting as a receptor within a neuron.

questions.explanation: Dendrites are branch-like structures extending from the cell body of a neuron. They receive signals from other neurons and act as receptors for neurotransmitters, allowing them to receive incoming signals and pass them to the cell body.

questions.text: Which of the following membrane potential depicts hyperpolarization?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A membrane potential of –50 mV is depolarization, because it is less negative than the resting potential (–70 mV), meaning the membrane moved closer to zero.
Hyperpolarization is when the membrane potential becomes more negative than the resting level (–70 mV). At –90 mV, this occurs due to extra K⁺ leaving or Cl⁻ entering the cell.
A membrane potential of +50 mV is not hyperpolarization — it is depolarisation, because the inside of the cell has become positive compared to the resting potential (–70 mV).
This is the resting membrane potential of a neuron. The inside of the neuron is negatively charged, but not as negatively charged as it would be in a hyperpolarized state.

questions.explanation: Hyperpolarization is a state in which the membrane potential becomes more negative than the normal resting potential of about –70 mV. For example, when the potential reaches –80 mV or –90 mV, the inside of the cell is said to be hyperpolarized. This usually occurs due to the continued outward movement of potassium ions or the inward movement of chloride ions, making the interior of the cell more negative than usual.

questions.text: Our most unconscious behaviors are controlled by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Thalamus: The thalamus is a relay station for sensory information. It is not involved in unconscious behaviors.

Our most unconscious behaviors are controlled by the limbic system. The limbic system is a group of structures in the brain that are involved in emotions, motivation, and memory. It is located near the center of the brain and includes the amygdala, hippocampus, hypothalamus, and cingulate cortex.

Cranial nerves: Cranial nerves are responsible for transmitting sensory information from the head and neck to the brain and for controlling muscles in the head and neck. They are not involved in unconscious behaviors.
Cerebral cortex: The cerebral cortex is the outer layer of the brain. It is involved in conscious thought, perception, and language. It is not involved in unconscious behaviors.

questions.explanation: Our most unconscious behaviors are controlled by the limbic system. The limbic system is a group of structures in the brain that are involved in emotions, motivation, and memory. It is located near the center of the brain and includes the amygdala, hippocampus, hypothalamus, and cingulate cortex.

questions.text: One that connects both cerebral hemispheres is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Corpus luteum: The corpus luteum is a temporary endocrine gland that forms in the ovary after ovulation. It produces the hormone progesterone, which is essential for maintaining pregnancy.
Corpus striatum: The corpus striatum is a group of nuclei in the brain that are involved in movement, learning, and memory. It is made up of the caudate nucleus, putamen, and globus pallidus.
The structure that connects both cerebral hemispheres is called the corpus callosum. It is a large band of nerve fibers that allows the two hemispheres to communicate with each other.
Tracts: Tracts are bundles of nerve fibers that connect different parts of the brain. They allow different parts of the brain to communicate with each other and coordinate their activities.

questions.explanation: The corpus callosum is a large bundle of nerve fibers that connects the left and right hemispheres of the brain, allowing them to communicate and share information. Composed of millions of myelinated axons, it is the largest white matter structure in the brain. Its primary function is interhemispheric communication, which is essential for integrating sensory information, coordinating motor and cognitive functions, and supporting abstract thought and problem-solving abilities.

questions.text: Which sentence demonstrates correct subject-verb agreement?
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Incorrect, because "cat" is singular, so it should be "is" instead of "are."
Incorrect, because "cats" is plural, so it should be "are" instead of "is."
Incorrect, because "has" is used for singular subjects, and "cats" is plural. It should be "are sleeping."
Correct, as "cats" is plural, and "are" matches the plural subject.

questions.explanation: Correct, as "cats" is plural, and "are" matches the plural subject.

questions.text: Read the following passage to answer the given question:Nobody knows when fiction started. Perhaps the first storyteller was a prehistoric mother trying to explain the world to her children, or maybe it was a hunter talking about his adventure around the campfire. Who can tell? What we do know, though, is that storytelling was a purely oral activity around 800 BC. Myths and tales were passed down by word of mouth and had to be popularized by each new generation of storytellers. This oral tradition is the only example of Homer, a professional storyteller who lived in the eighth century BC.The contextual meaning of "fiction" is
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: According to given passage, the contextual meaning of fiction should be narrative.Hence, option A is correct.
Textbook: "Fiction" is not related to factual, instructional material like a textbook.
Prose: While fiction is often written in prose, this term doesn't fully capture the context of "fiction" as a made-up narrative.
Truth: "Fiction" refers to imaginative stories, not to facts or truth.

questions.explanation: According to given passage, the contextual meaning of fiction should be narrative. Hence, option A is correct.

questions.text: Read the following passage to answer the given question:Nobody knows when fiction started. Perhaps the first storyteller was a prehistoric mother trying to explain the world to her children, or maybe it was a hunter talking about his adventure around the campfire. Who can tell? What we do know, though, is that storytelling was a purely oral activity around 800 BC. Myths and tales were passed down by word of mouth and got to be popularized by each new generation of storytellers. This oral tradition is the only example of Homer, a professional storyteller who lived in the eighth century BC.Myths were passed down by:
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: "Written scriptures": The passage mentions oral storytelling, not written scriptures.
"Carved activity": There's no mention of myths being passed down through carvings or physical inscriptions in the passage.
According to line 4 of given passage, myths were passed down by word of mouth. Hence, option C is correct.
"Inscribed activity": The passage does not refer to myths being passed down through inscriptions or writing.

questions.explanation: According to line 4 of given passage, myths were passed down by word of mouth. Hence, option C is correct.

questions.text: Read the following passage to answer the given question:Nobody knows when fiction started. Perhaps the first storyteller was a prehistoric mother trying to explain the world to her children, or maybe it was a hunter talking about his adventure around the campfire. Who can tell? What we do know, though, is that storytelling was a purely oral activity around 800 BC. Myths and tales were passed down by word of mouth and had to be popularized by each new generation of storytellers. This oral tradition is the only example of Homer, a professional storyteller who lived in the eighth century BC.It is stressed in the passage that storytelling:
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: "Began as a written activity": The passage emphasizes storytelling as an oral tradition, not a written one, especially in prehistoric times.

options.text: Became far more popular with the invention of writing
options.explanation: "Became far more popular with the invention of writing": The passage doesn’t mention writing’s role in making storytelling more popular.

options.text: Was first introduced by Homer in ancient times
options.explanation: "Was first introduced by Homer in ancient times": The passage states Homer was a storyteller, but it doesn’t claim he introduced storytelling.
According to 1st line of given passage, the story-telling possibly began in prehistoric times. Hence, option D is correct.

questions.explanation: According to 1st line of given passage, the story-telling possibly began in prehistoric times. Hence, option D is correct.

questions.text: The word 'RELUCTANT' means:
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This is the correct option."Reluctant" is an adjective that describes someone who is:1. Unwilling to do something2. Hesitant or slow to act3. Unenthusiastic or lacking eagerness
This word refers to something that is happening or existing now.
This word means being worthy of notice or attention, often due to being exceptional or impressive.
This word describes behavior that is impolite, discourteous, or rough.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is A: Hesitant."Reluctant" is an adjective that describes someone who is: 1. Unwilling to do something 2. Hesitant or slow to act 3. Unenthusiastic or lacking eagerness

questions.text: The synonym of "Apparel" is:
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: It is not the correct option, as "dubious" means doubtful or uncertain, while "apparel" refers to clothing.
Disguise refers to camouflage or a cover-up, while apparel means clothing.
The statement is accurate since "apparel" refers to clothing or attire.
Apparel refers to clothing, whereas an apron serves to cover or protect clothing. So it's not a correct option.

questions.explanation: Apparel: A covering designed to be worn on a person's body.Hence, "dress" is the most suitable option.

questions.text: The antonym of " Endeavour" is:
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: It is the correct option because inaction is the antonym of endeavor, which means effort or labor.
It's not the correct option because it is not an antonym of 'endeavor.' It means the opposite, which is neither a synonym nor an antonym of 'endeavor.'
It's incorrect because it's a synonym of endeavor, while the question asks for its antonym.
It is not a correct option because it is neither a synonym nor an antonym of "endeavor."

questions.explanation: "Endeavour" means to make an effort or attempt to achieve something, while "Inaction" means the absence of action or effort.

questions.text: Complete the series A3.3, B6.6, C9.9, _?
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.explanation: 3.3+3.3=6.6 6.6+3.3=9.9 9.9+3.3=13.23.3 is added in each step.Hence, D is the correct option.

questions.text: Statement and argument:Statement: Should the public sector undertakings be allowed to adopt a hire-and-fire policy?Arguments:(I) Yes, as this will help the public sector undertakings to get rid of non-performing employees and will also help to reward the performing employees.(II) No, the management may not be able to implement the policy in an unbiased manner and the employees will suffer due to the high-handedness of the management.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.explanation: Hire and fire policy' implies 'taking up the performing employees and discarding the non-performing ones'. Clearly, such a policy would stand out to encourage employees to work hard and devotedly to retain their jobs and thus enhance productivity and profitability of the organizations. Argument II seems to be vague in the light of this.

This option is incorrect because both arguments have valid points, and it is not evident that one argument is significantly stronger than the other. Argument II raises concerns about potential management biases in implementing the policy.

options.text: Both arguments I and II are strong
options.explanation: Both arguments have valid points, and it is not evident that one argument is significantly stronger than the other. Argument I is strong because it highlights a practical benefit of adopting a hire-and-fire policy: removing non-performing employees can increase efficiency, and rewarding performing employees can improve motivation. This argument is logical and directly supports the statement. Argument II is also strong because it presents a real and reasonable concern. In many public sector undertakings, management may misuse such policies due to bias or corruption. This may lead to injustice and insecurity among employees. This argument is also logical and relevant.

options.text: Neither argument I nor II is strong
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because both arguments have reasonable points, even though they also have some flaws. It is not accurate to dismiss both arguments entirely.

questions.explanation: Both arguments have valid points, and it is not evident that one argument is significantly stronger than the other. Argument I is strong because it highlights a practical benefit of adopting a hire-and-fire policy: removing non-performing employees can increase efficiency, and rewarding performing employees can improve motivation. This argument is logical and directly supports the statement. Argument II is also strong because it presents a real and reasonable concern. In many public sector undertakings, management may misuse such policies due to bias or corruption. This may lead to injustice and insecurity among employees. This argument is also logical and relevant.

questions.text: Statement and Argument Statement: Should admission to the professional degrees in Pakistan be given only on merit without any concession to any particular group of students? Arguments:(I) Yes. This will improve the quality of the professional institutes and degrees as they will be able to complete the degree successfully. (II) No. This will keep a large number of socially and economically backward students out of the reach of professional institutes and degrees.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.explanation: Argument II is also very strong.

options.text: If only argument II is strong
options.explanation: Argument I is also strong.

options.text: If both I and II are strong.
options.explanation: According to the statement I only all the hardworking students will be able to avail the admission in the professional degree hence there will be almost 100% course completion and success rate. But statement II also has a strong argument i.e. that if no concessions are made and admissions are solely on the base of merit then the socially and economically backward students won't be able to enroll in these professional degrees. So option C is correct.

options.text: If neither I nor II is strong
options.explanation: Both arguments are very strong.

questions.explanation: According to the statement I only all the hardworking students will be able to avail the admission in the professional degree hence there will be almost 100% course completion and success rate. But statement II also has a strong argument i.e. that if no concessions are made and admissions are solely on the base of merit then the socially and economically backward students won't be able to enroll in these professional degrees. So option C is correct.
